notable.notable_blurb: physician, Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Patient — landmark US medical ethics
narrative_blocks.body: His 1849 book Physician and Patient has been described as the most original United States contribution to medical ethics in the nineteenth century. It argued for honesty with patients, condemned medical deception even when well-intentioned, and shaped American physician-patient communication norms for a generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After twenty-three years practicing medicine in Norwich, Connecticut, he was chosen in 1852 to succeed Dr. Eli Ives as Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. He held the chair until his death, combining teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.

narrative_blocks.header: Vice President of American Medical Association, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected Vice President of the American Medical Association in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine. The AMA at that time was the leading professional voice for orthodox medicine against homeopathy, eclectic medicine, and the prison sanitary movement.

narrative_blocks.header: Worthington Hooker School named for him
narrative_blocks.body: The Worthington Hooker School in New Haven's East Rock neighborhood still bears his name and is part of the city's public school system. The school was named in recognition of his Yale professorship and his presence in New Haven's intellectual establishment.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on 31 January 1855 to Henrietta Whitney Edwards — daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards — also reunited two branches of the Hooker family. Henrietta descended from Mary Hooker (1673-1740) through her great-great-grandmother Sarah Pierpont and her great-great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont, making her and Worthington both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Shaped by Silliman at Yale, Müller in Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: His Yale years (graduated 1825) put him under Benjamin Silliman, the chemistry and natural history professor who championed empirical observation as the basis of American science. Later study of the German anatomist Johannes Müller's comparative physiology shaped Hooker's textbooks on Human Physiology and his rationalist defense of evidence-based therapeutics against homeopathy.

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers framed Eli Ives at Yale, 1817-1852
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother George took the same Yale M.D. degree in 1817 and apprenticed directly with Dr. Eli Ives, then Yale's Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine. Thirty-five years later in 1852, Worthington was chosen to succeed Ives in that same Yale chair when Ives retired — making the Hooker brothers the bookends of one of New Haven's longest medical legacies.

education.notes: Medical studies in Philadelphia and Cambridge; Harvard M.D. 1829.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Public elementary and middle school in New Haven CT named after him; original 180 Canner Street building erected 1900; new middle school campus opened 2009.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, CT — gravestone reads 'Beloved Physician'

career.notes: Practiced medicine in Norwich, Connecticut for 23 years before his Yale appointment. His wife Mary Ingersoll died here in early 1853 just months after the family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: Professor of Theory and Practice of Medicine
career.notes: Succeeded Dr. Eli Ives in the chair when Ives resigned. Held the professorship until his death; combined teaching with active medical practice and continued publication.
Elected VP of the AMA in 1864 — among the highest national positions in mid-19th-century American medicine.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: School in New Haven named for her husband Worthington Hooker; built 1900, six years after her own 1894 death.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, with husband Worthington

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: She and Worthington Hooker (H01407) were both seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker when they married 31 January 1855 — Worthington via the direct John Hooker paternal line, Henrietta via Sarah Pierpont (HD0418) and Rev. James Pierpont's marriage to Mary Hooker (H00034) in the 1690s. The couple had no children together, leaving the dual descent uncombined in any biological line.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Connecticut Governor Henry W. Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Gov. Henry Waggaman Edwards (HD2045) served Connecticut as governor 1833-34 and 1835-38, after representing the state in both the US Senate (1823-27) and House of Representatives (1819-23). His mother Frances Ogden was a cousin of Aaron Burr, making Henrietta a cousin once removed of the third Vice President of the United States.

cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law; Richest man in old Hampshire County

narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1782 and settled at Springfield, MA, where he practiced law and rose to Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and then Judge of Probate at his death. He was a Deacon of the First Congregational Church of Springfield throughout this period.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of American Board for Foreign Missions
narrative_blocks.body: He was an active member of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM), the first US foreign-missions organization, founded 1810 in Williamstown MA. The Board sent New England missionaries to India, the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii), and Native American tribes — a defining institution of New England Federalist evangelical culture.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father built this house, later moved to Historic Deerfield.

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, beside her husband Judge John Hooker

narrative_blocks.body: She was born 13 December 1764 to Col. Josiah Dwight — Yale 1736, owner of an iron foundry, a potash manufactory, and 5,000 acres of Hampshire County land — and to Elizabeth Buckminster of Brookfield. Her father died in 1768 leaving an estate worth £9,458 when she was four; her mother survived to 1798.

On 18 December 1790 in Springfield she married John Hooker, Yale 1782 graduate and son of the Rev. John of Northampton. She bore him ten children between 1791 and 1808, including Dr. Worthington Hooker (H01407) and Elizabeth Dwight Hooker Packard (H01403).

sources.label: NPG Catalog of American Portraits CT140017

cross_connections.notes: Aaron Burr Jr. also studied at Litchfield before Reeve married his sister — the school trained multiple Hooker descendants.
cross_connections.display_label: Studied at Litchfield Law School, founded by Tapping Reeve

notable.notable_blurb: 27th and 29th Governor of Connecticut; US Senator

narrative_blocks.header: Twice Governor of Connecticut, US Senator, Edwards grandson
narrative_blocks.detail: Yale-educated (Princeton B.A. 1797) and a Litchfield Law School graduate, Edwards also served as US Representative 1819-1823 and as Connecticut State Senate president and Speaker of the Connecticut House. His mother Frances Ogden (X00654) was from a prominent New Jersey family; his father Pierpont Edwards (H00386) was a Yale judge and Continental Congress delegate. His sister Henrietta Frances Edwards (HD2046) married Eli Whitney, inventor of the cotton gin. His niece Henrietta Whitney Edwards (X00160) married Worthington Hooker (H01407) — a previously unrecognized convergence of the Hooker and Edwards lines. (Wikipedia; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Waggaman Edwards served as the 27th and 29th Governor of Connecticut (1833-1834 and 1835-1838) and as a US Senator (1823-1827), making him the most politically prominent of Pierpont Edwards's sons.

narrative_blocks.header: Trained at Litchfield Law School by Tapping Reeve
narrative_blocks.detail: The school founded by his relative-by-marriage trained some of the most prominent American lawyers and politicians of the early republic — among them Aaron Burr Jr., who studied under Reeve before Reeve married Burr's sister. The Litchfield Law School network running through the Hooker-Edwards-Burr family is one of the densest institutional cross-connections in the entire tree. (EH 1909; Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Edwards studied law at Litchfield Law School, founded by Tapping Reeve — who had married Sally Burr Reeve, sister of Aaron Burr Jr. and great-aunt of Edwards himself.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Henrietta married fellow Hooker descendant Worthington Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Henrietta Whitney Edwards married Worthington Hooker, bringing his Edwards-Pierpont line back into convergence with the Hooker descent. Their marriage produced a Hooker double-descent generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Funded the Percival geological survey during second governorship
narrative_blocks.body: During his second governorship in 1835, Connecticut became the first state to fund a comprehensive geological survey. He appointed James Gates Percival — a fellow Hooker descendant — as state geologist; the resulting survey took seven years across two-mile intervals.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law as Tapping Reeve's grand-nephew through the Burr connection.
